# Could AI-designed proteins be weaponized? Scientists lay out safety guidelines

Search author on:
[PubMed](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=search&term=Ewen+Callaway)
 [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.co.uk/scholar?as_q=&btnG=Search+Scholar&as_sauthors=%22Ewen%2BCallaway%22)

Could proteins designed by artificial intelligence (AI) ever be used as bioweapons? In the hope of heading off this possibility — as well as the prospect of burdensome government regulation — researchers today launched an initiative calling for the safe and ethical use of protein design.

--- SOURCE 4: The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8

SUMMARY:
The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool | AI and Ethics  In this article, we briefly introduce AI and explain how it can be used in research, examine some of the ethical issues raised when using it, and offer nine recommendations for responsible use, including: (1) Researchers are responsible for identifying, describing, reducing, and controlling AI-related biases and random errors; (2) Researchers should disclose, describe, and explain their use of AI in research, including its limitations, in language that can be understood by non-experts; (3) Researchers should engage with impacted communities, populations, and other stakeholders concerning the use of AI in research to obtain their advice and assistance and address their interests and concerns, such as issues related to bias; (4) Researchers who use synthetic data should (a) indicate which parts of the data are synthetic; (b) clearly label the synthetic data; (c) describe how the data were generated; and (d) explain how and why the data were used; (5) AI systems should not be named as authors, inventors, or copyright holders but their contributions to research should be disclosed and described; (6) Education and mentoring in responsible conduct of research should include discussion of ethical use of AI.

FULL CONTENT:
The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool | AI and Ethics

 The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool 

[Download PDF](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8.pdf)

*   [David B. Resnik](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#auth-David_B_-Resnik-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-5139-9555](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5139-9555)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Aff1)&
*   [Mohammad Hosseini](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#auth-Mohammad-Hosseini-Aff2-Aff3)[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Aff2),[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Aff3)

*   [Explore all metrics](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Using artificial intelligence (AI) in research offers many important benefits for science and society but also creates novel and complex ethical issues. While these ethical issues do not necessitate changing established ethical norms of science, they require the scientific community to develop new guidance for the appropriate use of AI. In this article, we briefly introduce AI and explain how it can be used in research, examine some of the ethical issues raised when using it, and offer nine recommendations for responsible use, including: (1) Researchers are responsible for identifying, describing, reducing, and controlling AI-related biases and random errors; (2) Researchers should disclose, describe, and explain their use of AI in research, including its limitations, in language that can be understood by non-experts; (3) Researchers should engage with impacted communities, populations, and other stakeholders concerning the use of AI in research to obtain their advice and assistance and address their interests and concerns, such as issues related to bias; (4) Researchers who use synthetic data should (a) indicate which parts of the data are synthetic; (b) clearly label the synthetic data; (c) describe how the data were generated; and (d) explain how and why the data were used; (5) AI systems should not be named as authors, inventors, or copyright holders but their contributions to research should be disclosed and described; (6) Education and mentoring in responsible conduct of research should include discussion of ethical use of AI.

1 Introduction: exponential growth in the use of artificial intelligence in scientific research
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In just a few years, artificial intelligence (AI) has taken the world of scientific research by storm. AI tools have been used to perform or augment a variety of scientific tasks, including[Footnote 1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Fn1):

Methods Ecol. Nature 608, 14–16 (2022)"), [43](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR43 "Castelvecchi, D.: AI chatbot shows surprising talent for predicting chemical properties and reactions.                   https://doi.org/10.3390/ma16175927
                  
                "), [37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR37 "Brumfiel, G.: New proteins, better batteries: Scientists are using AI to speed up discoveries. NPR (2023). 
                  https://www.npr.org/sections/health-shots/2023/10/12/1205201928/artificial-intelligence-ai-scientific-discoveries-proteins-drugs-solar

                  https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-00347-7

Nat. Biomed. Eng. 5, 493–497 (2021)"), [200](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR200 "Savage, N.: Synthetic data could be better than real data. Nature (2023). 
                  https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-023-01445-8
                  
                ")].

Nature (2018). 
                  https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-018-06617-5

NPJ Digit. Nature 619, 443–444 (2023)"), [54](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR54 "Conroy, G.: How ChatGPT and other AI tools could disrupt scientific publishing. Nature (2023). 
                  https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-023-03144-w
                  
                "), [82](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR82 "Godwin, R.C., Bryant, A.S., Wagener, B.M., Ness, T.J., DeBerryJJ, H.L.L., Graves, S.H., Archer, A.C., Melvin, R.L.: IRB-draft-generator: a generative AI tool to streamline the creation of institutional review board applications. SoftwareX 25, 101601 (2024)"), [163](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR163 "Nordling, L.: How ChatGPT is transforming the postdoc experience. *   Reviewing scientific papers and other research outputs [[53](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR53 "Conroy, G.: Scientists used ChatGPT to generate an entire paper from scratch—but is it any good? Nature 619, 443–444 (2023)"), [54](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR54 "Conroy, G.: How ChatGPT and other AI tools could disrupt scientific publishing. Nature (2023).

                  https://deal.town/the-chronicle-of-higher-education/academe-today-publishing-decisions-are-increasingly-aided-by-ai-but-thats-not-always-obvious-PK2J5KUC4
                  
                ")].

The applications of AI in scientific research appears to be limitless, and in the next decade AI is likely to completely transform the process of scientific discovery and innovation [[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR6 "Alvarado, R.: Should we replace radiologists with deep learning? Bioethics 36(2), 121–133 (2022)"),[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR7 "Alvarado, R.: What kind of trust does AI deserve, if any? AI Ethics (2022). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-022-00224-x
                  
                "),[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR8 "Alvarado, R.: Computer simulations as scientific instruments. Found. Sci. 27(3), 1183–1205 (2022)"),[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR9 "Alvarado, R.: AI as an epistemic technology. Sci. Eng. Ethics 29, 32 (2023)"), [105](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR105 "Humphreys, P.: Extending Ourselves: Computational Science, Empiricism, and Scientific Method. Oxford University Press, New York (2004)"), [201](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR201 "Schmidt, E.: This is how AI will transform the way science gets done. MIT Technology Review (2023). 
                  https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/07/05/1075865/eric-schmidt-ai-will-transform-science/#:~:text=AI%20can%20also%20spread%20the,promising%20candidates%20for%20new%20drugs
                  
                ")].

Although using AI in scientific research has steadily grown, ethical guidance has lagged far behind. With the exception of using AI to draft or edit scientific papers (see discussion in Sect.[7.6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Sec21)), most codes and policies do not explicitly address ethical issues related to using AI in scientific research. For example, the 2023 revision of the European Code of Conduct for Research Integrity [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR4 "All European Academies.: The European Code of Conduct for Research Integrity, Revised Edition 2023 (2023). 
                  https://allea.org/code-of-conduct/
                  
                ")] briefly discusses the importance of transparency. The code stipulates that researchers should report “their results and methods including the use of external services or AI and automated tools” (Ibid., p. 7) and considers “hiding the use of AI or automated tools in the creation of content or drafting of publications” as a violation of research integrity (Ibid. p. 10). One of the most thorough and up-to-date institutional documents, the National Institutes of Health Guidelines and Policies for the Conduct of Research provides guidance for using AI to write and edit manuscripts but not for other tasks [[158](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR158 "National Institutes of Health.: Guidelines for the Conduct of Research in the Intramural Program of the NIH (2023). 
                  https://oir.nih.gov/system/files/media/file/2023-11/guidelines-conduct_research.pdf
                  
                ")].[Footnote 2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Fn2) Codes of AI ethics, such as UNESCO’s [[223](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR223 "UNESCO.: Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2024). 
                  https://www.unesco.org/en/artificial-intelligence/recommendation-ethics
                  
                ")] Ethics of Artificial Intelligence and the Office of Science and Technology Policy’s [[168](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR168 "Office of Science and Technology Policy: Federal research misconduct policy. Fed. Reg. 65(235), 76260–76264 (2000)"), [169](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR169 "Office and Science and Technology Policy.: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights (2022). 
                  https://www.whitehouse.gov/ostp/ai-bill-of-rights/
                  
                ")] Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, provide useful guidance for the development and use of AI in general without including specific guidance concerning the development and use of AI in scientific research [[215](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR215 "Tambornino, L., Lanzerath, D., Rodrigues, R., Wright, D.: SIENNA D4.3: survey of REC approaches and codes for Artificial Intelligence & Robotics (2019). 
                  https://zenodo.org/records/4067990
                  
                ")].

There is therefore a gap in ethical and policy guidance concerning AI use in scientific research that needs to be filled to promote its appropriate use. Moreover, the need for guidance is urgent because using AI raises novel epistemological and ethical issues related to objectivity, reproducibility, transparency, accountability, responsibility, and trust in science [[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR9 "Alvarado, R.: AI as an epistemic technology. Sci. Eng. Ethics 29, 32 (2023)"), [102](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR102 "Hosseini, M., Resnik, D.B., Holmes, K.: The ethics of disclosing the use of artificial intelligence in tools writing scholarly manuscripts. Res. Ethics (2023). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/17470161231180449
                  
                ")]. In this paper, we will examine important questions related to AI’s impact on ethics of science. We will argue that while the use of AI does not require a radical change in the ethical norms of science, it will require the scientific community to develop new guidance for the appropriate use of AI. To defend this thesis, we will provide an overview of AI and an account of ethical norms of science, and then we will discuss the implications of AI for ethical norms of science and offer recommendations for its appropriate use.

2 What is AI?
-------------

AI can be defined as “a technical and scientific field devoted to the engineered system that generates outputs such as content, forecasts, recommendations or decisions for a given set of human-defined objectives [[114](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR114 "International Organization for Standardization.: What is AI? (2024). 
                  https://www.iso.org/artificial-intelligence/what-is-ai#:~:text=Artificial%20intelligence%20is%20%E2%80%9Ca%20technical,%2FIEC%2022989%3A2022%5D
                  
                ")].” AI is a _subfield_ within the discipline of computer science [[144](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR144 "McCarthy, J.: What is artificial intelligence? (2007). 
                  https://www-formal.stanford.edu/jmc/whatisai.pdf
                  
                ")]. However, the term ‘AI’ is also commonly used to refer to _technologies (or tools)_ that can perform human tasks that require intelligence, such as perception, judgment, reasoning, or decision-making. We will use both senses of ‘AI’ in this paper, depending on the context.

While electronic calculators, cell phone apps, and programs that run on personal computers can perform functions associated with intelligence, they are not generally considered to be AI because they do not “learn” from the data [[108](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR108 "IBM.: What is AI? (2023). 
                  https://www.ibm.com/topics/artificial-intelligence
                  
                ")]. As discussed below, AI systems can learn from the data insofar as they can adapt their programming in response to input data. While applying the term ‘learning’ to a machine may seem misleadingly anthropomorphic, it does make sense to say that a machine can learn if learning is regarded as a change in response to information about the environment [[151](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR151 "Mitchell, M.: Artificial Intelligence. Picador, New York (2019)")]. Many different entities can learn in this sense of the term, including the immune system, which changes after being exposed to molecular information about pathogens, foreign objects, and other things that provoke an immune response.

This paper will focus on what is commonly referred to as narrow (or weak) AI, which is already being extensively used in science. Narrow AI has been designed and developed to do a specific task, such as playing chess, modelling complex phenomena, or identifying possible brain tumors in diagnostic images [[151](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR151 "Mitchell, M.: Artificial Intelligence. Picador, New York (2019)")]. See Fig.[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Fig1).[Footnote 3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#Fn3) Other types of AI discussed in the literature include broad AI (also known as artificial general intelligence or AGI), which is a machine than can perform multiple tasks requiring human-like intelligence; and artificial consciousness (AC), which is a form of AGI with characteristics widely considered to be essential for consciousness [[162](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR162 "Ng, G.W., Leung, W.C.: Strong artificial intelligence and consciousness. J. Artif. Intell. Conscious. 7(1), 63–72 (2020)"), [219](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00493-8#ref-CR219 "Turing.: Complete analysis of artificial intelligence vs artificial consciousness (2023). 
                  https://www.turing.com/kb/complete-analysis-of-artificial-intelligence-vs-artificial-consciousness
                  
                ")]. Because there are significant technical and conceptual obstacles to developing AGI and AC, it may be years before machines have this degree of human-like intel

Protein-folding prediction can help with disease understanding and drug development, but also has potential for malicious use, creating a dual-use dilemma.

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI's role in protein folding simulations raises ethical concerns regarding data bias, transparency, and the potential misuse of AI-designed proteins, necessitating responsible guidelines.
Researchers must ensure ethical use of computational resources, addressing AI-related biases and errors, and promoting transparency in methodologies to foster trust and integrity.
AI-driven protein folding advancements can revolutionize healthcare by enhancing drug discovery and disease understanding, but ethical frameworks must prevent dual-use for harmful applications.
